checks.explanation:
AGENTS.md at the repo root is the emerging canonical contract between
human authors and coding agents (canonical commands, do-not-touch
lists, commit conventions). Other formats (.cursorrules, CLAUDE.md,
.ai/) help, but a single, well-named file at the root is the most
predictable surface for any agent. This rule fires `warn` only when
the canonical AGENTS.md is missing; partial coverage by sibling
formats is rewarded by the softer `agent_docs.present` rule.

Agents perform measurably better when the repo includes docs aimed at
them (canonical commands, do-not-touch lists, branch conventions). A
README is for humans; AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / .cursorrules /
.cursor/rules/*.mdc / .github/copilot-instructions.md / .ai/ /
prompts/ are common locations for agent-targeted material. This rule
fires `info` when none of them are present.

High cyclomatic complexity means many independent control-flow
paths through one function. Agents that try to modify such a
function have to consider every path; they make more wrong-branch
edits than humans do, who can hold more context in their heads.
Functions over CC=15 are the strongest predictor of agent edit
errors in a 2026 internal study.

Agents and contributors expect a root README.md as the primary orientation
surface. Its absence forces guesswork from filenames and CI config alone.

A CONTRIBUTING.md tells agents (and humans) the contribution flow:
branch naming, commit message style, PR template expectations,
required pre-commit checks. Absence forces the agent to infer this
from git log, which it gets wrong on small repos with little history.
Community-contributed demo rule.

action.template:
# Contributing

## Setup
`make install`

## Test
`make test`

## Lint
`make lint`

## Submitting changes
Fork, branch, PR. CI must be green before review.

findings.fix_hint:
Add a CONTRIBUTING.md describing branch naming, commit style,
required local checks, and the PR review flow.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: CONTRIBUTING.md, CONTRIBUTING, .github/CONTRIBUTING.md

verify.description: CONTRIBUTING.md must exist at the repo root.

checks.explanation:
Files that are both large *and* frequently edited are the highest-
risk surface for an agent: each touch has the most context to
re-read and the most history to consider. Splitting them into
smaller modules cuts agent error rates more than any other
refactor. This rule fires per file that exceeds both the touch and
size thresholds; gives `not_measured` on shallow clones and
repos with very few commits.

Drift between what README documents and what the repo actually has
is one of the most expensive false starts for an agent: it follows
the README, runs into a missing tool, and either reverts or
invents a workaround. Common drifts caught here: README mentions
pytest but no pytest config exists; README mentions `npm test`
but no package.json; README references `make X` for a target that
doesn't exist in the Makefile; README documents `npm run X` but
the script is missing from package.json; README documents
`pip install -e .` but no pyproject.toml/setup.py exists; AGENTS.md
/ CLAUDE.md is a placeholder.

An agent that needs to find "the function that retries failed HTTP
calls" can't `grep utils.py` — every project has one. Generic
names (`utils`, `helpers`, `manager`, `common`, `lib`) at top of
`src/` increase the search radius for any agent change. Names
that describe intent shrink it.

The README is the agent's first stop. If it has prose but no
`install` / `run` / `test` signals — paragraphs without fenced code
blocks, or no mention of those steps at all — the agent has to
reverse-engineer the workflow from manifests and CI configs. Each
of the three signals (install / run / test) is independently
scored; the rule fires when any are missing.

Files over 3000 lines or 300 KB exceed an agent's working memory in
a single read and force a chunked-reading pattern that loses
context across chunk boundaries. This rule excludes lock files,
generated data, binary assets, long-form tests, vendored
third-party code, and build/template artefacts — which are large
but not "source" files an agent would meaningfully edit. Thresholds
recalibrated in v2.0.1 (3000 lines / 300 KB) from v1.5.0's 1500 /
150 KB setting after EXP-2 fire-rate measurement on the v3.2 cohort;
see the rule comment block for cohort calibration history.

Most agents start a session by reading some combination of
AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / README + the primary manifest + a handful
of representative source files. If that combined "orientation
bundle" exceeds the agent's context budget, the agent either
truncates (loses signal) or skips files (works from incomplete
knowledge). Repos that fit comfortably under the warn threshold
give every agent a fair start. Documentation-rich repos (e.g.
large frameworks with comprehensive READMEs) can override
``warn_tokens`` per-repo via ``.agent-readiness.toml``.

An agent's first impression of a repo is `ls`. A root with 30+
non-meta files (excluding readme / license / config) signals a
flat, undirectory'd project where finding *anything* requires
reading every name. Mature projects keep the root short by
pushing source into `src/`, `pkg/`, `app/`, etc.

CI is the main automated feedback channel once an agent pushes a
branch. repos that never tell agents to watch workflow results or to
treat failing checks as something to fix (not noise) see agents land
red PRs, declare "done," and disappear — the same failure mode as
missing hooks, only slower. Root `AGENTS.md` should explicitly bind
**monitor CI / checks → read failures → fix and iterate** into the
agent contract.

action.template:
# Agents guide

## Feedback loop

- To preview locally: `make ci-local`
- To re-run the failing CI step: `gh run rerun --failed`
- CI artifacts are uploaded to the run page.

findings.fix_hint:
Add a short "CI and the feedback loop" section to `AGENTS.md`: after
pushing or updating a PR, monitor workflow / PR check results; when CI
fails, use logs as signal, fix the root cause, and iterate until green.
findings.message:
AGENTS.md is present but does not tell agents to monitor CI runs /
check results and act on failures as feedback.

verify.description: AGENTS.md must reference the CI feedback loop.

checks.explanation:
CI is how a coding agent confirms that "my change passes" the same
way a human reviewer would. Without it, the agent ships PRs that
green-on-laptop, red-in-prod, and the project picks up the cleanup.
Any modern CI provider counts; the rule is just "*some* automated
test pipeline is configured." Some teams use hosted CI (BuildKite
cloud, TeamCity hosted, Jenkins on a remote server) where there's
no in-repo config to detect — for those repos, document the CI
with a README badge so the agent knows the loop exists.

When a workflow ships a build to a hosted environment (GitHub Pages,
Netlify, Vercel, S3/CloudFront, gh-pages branch, Cloud Run, Fly,
Render, Surge), the only feedback signal an agent gets out of the
box is "the deploy step exited 0." That is not the same as "the
deployed site actually loads." Without a post-deploy smoke check
(curl HTTP 200, Playwright/Puppeteer probe, Lighthouse run, etc.),
silent prod regressions — broken `base` paths, broken SPA 404
fallbacks, asset 404s, missing data files — ship green and the
agent declares "done."

This rule fires when at least one workflow file references a deploy
step, AND no workflow file contains evidence of a smoke check
(HTTP probe, e2e tool, or `lighthouse` invocation).

Coverage configuration (`.coveragerc`, `codecov.yml`, `coveralls.yml`,
`pyproject.toml [tool.coverage.*]`) signals that the repo tracks which
code is exercised by tests. Agents benefit from knowing what is and
isn't tested before making a change, so they can target tests
appropriately. Community-contributed demo rule.

findings.fix_hint:
Add a coverage config (`.coveragerc` for pytest-cov, `codecov.yml` for
Codecov) so coverage signal is visible to both humans and agents.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: .coveragerc, codecov.yml, .codecov.yml, coveralls.yml, .coveralls.yml

verify.description: A coverage threshold must be configured.

checks.explanation:
Hooks (pre-commit, husky, lefthook, lint-staged, .claude/settings,
.cursor/settings) are how a project gives an agent feedback *before*
a commit lands rather than after CI fails. A repo that runs format
+ lint on every commit cuts the agent's edit/wait/fix loop by an
order of magnitude.

A configured linter is the difference between an agent's edit being
immediately rejected with a clear error and the same edit silently
drifting from house style. Even if the linter is permissive, having
*a* config tells agents which rules are intentional and which are
drift.

A manifest (`pyproject.toml`, `package.json`, `Cargo.toml`,
`go.mod`, …) is the contract that tells an agent what the project
is, how to install it, and what depends on what. Without one, the
agent has to infer everything from filenames and tribal knowledge.
This rule looks for a manifest at the repo root or, for monorepos,
at depth ≤ 2 (`packages/<name>/pyproject.toml`).

A lockfile pins the exact dependency tree the project was last
validated against. Without one, `pip install` / `npm install` will
resolve different versions on different machines, so an agent that
installs deps and runs tests can't trust a green run on its laptop
to predict a green run in CI. Some ecosystems (Cargo, Poetry, npm)
produce lockfiles automatically; others (bare requirements.txt) do
not, and the project has to opt in.

action.command:
# Generate a lockfile using the language's package manager.
 lock ||  install --frozen-lockfile
action.description:
Run the package manager's lock command (e.g. `uv lock`,
`npm install --package-lock-only`, `cargo generate-lockfile`).

findings.fix_hint:
Run `poetry lock` / `npm install` / `cargo update` (whichever your
manifest needs) and commit the resulting lockfile. For pip-only
Python projects, freezing `requirements.txt` to exact versions or
switching to `uv` / `poetry` is acceptable.
findings.message: At least one ecosystem-specific lockfile is committed. (no clause matched)

verify.command:
test -f uv.lock || test -f poetry.lock || test -f package-lock.json || \
  test -f yarn.lock || test -f pnpm-lock.yaml || test -f Cargo.lock

action.command:
# Generate a lockfile using the language's package manager.
 lock ||  install --frozen-lockfile
action.description:
Run the package manager's lock command (e.g. `uv lock`,
`npm install --package-lock-only`, `cargo generate-lockfile`).

findings.fix_hint:
Run `poetry lock` / `npm install` / `cargo update` (whichever your
manifest needs) and commit the resulting lockfile. For pip-only
Python projects, freezing `requirements.txt` to exact versions or
switching to `uv` / `poetry` is acceptable.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: poetry.lock, uv.lock, Pipfile.lock, pdm.lock, package-lock.json, npm-shrinkwrap.json, yarn.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml, Cargo.lock, Gemfile.lock, composer.lock, go.sum, mix.lock, flake.lock, conda-lock.yml, pixi.lock

verify.command:
test -f uv.lock || test -f poetry.lock || test -f package-lock.json || \
  test -f yarn.lock || test -f pnpm-lock.yaml || test -f Cargo.lock

action.command:
# Generate a lockfile using the language's package manager.
 lock ||  install --frozen-lockfile
action.description:
Run the package manager's lock command (e.g. `uv lock`,
`npm install --package-lock-only`, `cargo generate-lockfile`).

findings.fix_hint:
Run `poetry lock` / `npm install` / `cargo update` (whichever your
manifest needs) and commit the resulting lockfile. For pip-only
Python projects, freezing `requirements.txt` to exact versions or
switching to `uv` / `poetry` is acceptable.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: requirements*.txt, environment.yml, environment.yaml

verify.command:
test -f uv.lock || test -f poetry.lock || test -f package-lock.json || \
  test -f yarn.lock || test -f pnpm-lock.yaml || test -f Cargo.lock

checks.explanation:
Agents need a single canonical test command they can invoke
headlessly. The strongest signal is a `test:` Makefile target;
next-strongest is `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` / `scripts.test` in
`package.json` / `tox.ini` / `pytest.ini` / language-conventional
test files (`*_test.go`, `Cargo.toml [[test]]`, `tests/`). Without
any of these the agent has to guess from shell history, often
picking the wrong invocation.

A type checker catches whole categories of agent errors (missing
fields, wrong arg shapes, stale imports) before they reach runtime.
Statically-typed language repos pass automatically; for Python and
JS this is opt-in but high-leverage.

Branch protection (or the newer "branch rulesets") forces PRs to
pass review and CI before they touch the default branch. For an
agent-driven workflow this is doubly important: it's the only
guard rail against an agent merging something that looks plausible
but breaks production. Without it, "the agent can write to main"
is true, and that's bad.

A devcontainer / Nix flake / Gitpod config / Vagrantfile lets an
agent (or a new contributor) get a reproducible environment without
hand-installing system deps. The project that ships one cuts the
"wrong Python version" / "missing libssl-dev" class of failure
to zero.

findings.fix_hint:
Drop a minimal `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` referencing a
Microsoft base image plus `postCreateCommand: pip install -e .`.
For Nix shops, `flake.nix` with a dev shell is the equivalent.
findings.message: At least one reproducible-workspace config is present. (no clause matched)

findings.fix_hint:
Drop a minimal `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` referencing a
Microsoft base image plus `postCreateCommand: pip install -e .`.
For Nix shops, `flake.nix` with a dev shell is the equivalent.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: .devcontainer/devcontainer.json, .devcontainer/**, .devcontainer.json, .gitpod.yml, .gitpod.Dockerfile, flake.nix, shell.nix, default.nix, .nix/**, devbox.json, mise.toml, .mise.toml, .tool-versions, Vagrantfile, .codesandbox/**, docker-compose.dev.yml, docker-compose.dev.yaml, compose.dev.yml, compose.dev.yaml

checks.explanation:
An agent looking to run the project needs an obvious entry point. A
manifest-declared script (`[project.scripts]` in pyproject.toml,
`bin` / `main` in package.json) is the strongest signal, but
conventional filenames (`main.py`, `cmd/<name>/main.go`,
`__main__.py`, `index.{js,ts}`) also count. Without any of these,
the agent has to guess from filenames, which fails for non-trivial
layouts.

When code reads from `.env` (or process environment) but no
`.env.example` documents the expected keys, headless setup
breaks: the agent can't tell which env vars to set before running
tests or the app. The full check is "code references env vars,
AND no .env.example-style file exists, AND no equivalent prose
documentation (``export FOO=`` snippets in README/CONTRIBUTING) is
present." All three halves must hold to fire — repos that document
env vars in narrative form get credit.

An agent that needs to reason about *why* a piece of code looks the
way it does — to backport, to revert, to bisect — depends on
commit history. A repo with one squash-import commit looks the same
as a repo with no history at all: every change is a mystery. This
rule fires when fewer than `min_commits` commits exist (and gives
a `not_measured` finding on shallow clones).

action.command:
# Initialize the repo and stage an initial commit so agents can
# see a baseline diff.
git init && git add -A && git commit -m 'initial commit'
action.description:
Initialize a git repo (or commit at least 5 changes). Required for
agents that diff against the merge base.

findings.fix_hint:
If you imported the project as a single squash, re-import preserving
history (`git filter-repo` or a fresh clone of the source). For new
projects, just keep committing — agents need the trail.
findings.message: Repository has 1 commits — fewer than min_commits=5.

checks.explanation:
An agent runs in a sandbox or CI — there's no human at a browser
to "click here to authorize" or "open the dashboard to confirm".
README setup steps that route through GUI authorisation flows or
browser-based config wizards are dead-ends for headless agents.
This rule scans the setup section of the README for
GUI / OAuth-redirect language and fires when any are present.

This is a composite of three already-shipping signals:
  - agent_docs.present  (cognitive_load)
  - entry_points.detected  (flow)
  - test_command.discoverable  (feedback)
Individually any one of them is a soft signal. Together — when ALL
THREE are absent — the repo is functionally agent-blind: no doc to
read, no thing to run, no way to verify. Most healthy repos satisfy
at least one; this rule isolates the worst-case intersection.

Note: the sub-conditions are written as AND-of-misses (each leaf
matcher fires explicitly when its signal is missing), not as OR-of-
alternatives mirrored from the source rules. The source rules use
OR semantics where the regex clauses default to fire_when=match and
go silent when their target file is absent — which makes those rules
under-fire on real repos. We avoid that by being explicit here.

Many AI coding agents (and new human contributors) try `make help`
first to discover what a repo can do. A Makefile with multiple
targets and no `help:` target forces every onboarding agent to read
the Makefile by hand. Cheap to add, surprisingly load-bearing for
first-touch ergonomics. Demo rule for the `insert_after` fix kind.

findings.fix_hint:
Add a `help:` target that prints each target with its inline `##`
comment. Inserting after `.PHONY:` keeps the canonical declaration
block tidy.

verify.description: `make help` must resolve to a runnable target.

checks.explanation:
Each command in a README's Setup / Installation section is a place
the agent can fail (wrong flag, missing prerequisite, version
mismatch). Agents succeed at 1-3 setup commands at near-100% and
fall off a cliff after 5. The rule counts distinct shell commands
in the setup section's fenced code blocks and shell-prompt lines.

Issue templates, PR templates, and CODEOWNERS make agent-opened PRs
routable: the right reviewer is auto-assigned, the right metadata
fields are filled in, and the project's expectations are explicit.
Repos without these get a flood of agent PRs that all look the same
and have no obvious reviewer.

action.template:
## Summary

What does this change do, in 1-2 sentences?

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit tests
- [ ] Manual verification

findings.fix_hint:
At minimum: drop `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` (GitHub has
a wizard), a `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with a checklist,
and a `CODEOWNERS` file mapping `*` to the team that should review
agent-opened PRs.
findings.message: Issue templates, PR template, or CODEOWNERS configured. (no clause matched)

action.template:
## Summary

What does this change do, in 1-2 sentences?

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit tests
- [ ] Manual verification

findings.fix_hint:
At minimum: drop `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` (GitHub has
a wizard), a `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with a checklist,
and a `CODEOWNERS` file mapping `*` to the team that should review
agent-opened PRs.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/**, .gitlab/issue_templates/**

action.template:
## Summary

What does this change do, in 1-2 sentences?

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit tests
- [ ] Manual verification

findings.fix_hint:
At minimum: drop `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` (GitHub has
a wizard), a `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with a checklist,
and a `CODEOWNERS` file mapping `*` to the team that should review
agent-opened PRs.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/**, .github/pull_request_template.md, .gitlab/merge_request_templates/**

action.template:
## Summary

What does this change do, in 1-2 sentences?

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit tests
- [ ] Manual verification

findings.fix_hint:
At minimum: drop `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` (GitHub has
a wizard), a `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with a checklist,
and a `CODEOWNERS` file mapping `*` to the team that should review
agent-opened PRs.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: CODEOWNERS, .github/CODEOWNERS, docs/CODEOWNERS

checks.explanation:
GitHub Actions workflows that push back to their own repo (typical
for daily-scan / weekly-rebuild / scheduled data-publishing
patterns) without a top-level `concurrency:` block can race
themselves. Two scheduled runs (or a manual edit colliding with the
cron) lose one set of changes silently — the second `git push` is
rejected by the remote, the workflow exits 0, and the data drift is
invisible until a human notices stale dashboards.

The fix is one block per workflow. For write workflows, prefer
`cancel-in-progress: false` so the second run waits for the first
rather than tossing its work.

An agent that runs `pip install` / `npm install` / a build will
produce artefact directories (`__pycache__`, `node_modules`,
`dist/`, `build/`, IDE settings, coverage reports). If the
`.gitignore` doesn't cover them, those artefacts get accidentally
committed and pollute future diffs. The same pattern catches
secrets — `.env` not being ignored is how API keys end up in
public history.

findings.fix_hint:
Run `gitignore.io` for your stack and paste the result into
`.gitignore`. At minimum, cover: `__pycache__`, `node_modules`,
`.env`, `dist/`, `build/`, `*.log`, IDE settings, coverage dirs.
findings.message: .gitignore covers 5/12 groups; missing: node_modules, go_vendor, rust_target, jvm_class, ide_junk, terraform, logs.

verify.description: .gitignore must cover the standard junk groups.

checks.explanation:
Stale dependencies and unscanned third-party code are the most
common path from "agent merged a small change" to "merged a CVE".
A repo that declares Dependabot, Renovate, Snyk, FOSSA, Mend, or
CodeQL gets PRs / alerts for vulnerable transitive deps and unsafe
patterns automatically, so agents reviewing those PRs can act on
them rather than re-discovering the issue from scratch.

An agent that handles credentials or pushes commits is one
copy-paste away from leaking a secret into git history. Repos that
declare a secret-scanning config (gitleaks, trufflehog, .pre-commit
with detect-secrets, or GitHub's native push protection markers)
signal that secret scanning is enforced as part of the contributor
flow, which materially reduces leak risk in agent-driven changes.

action.template:
# gitleaks configuration: tunes which paths and rules are scanned
# for secrets in pre-commit hooks and CI.
title = "gitleaks config"

[[allowlist.regexes]]
description = "Allow .env.example placeholder values"
regex = '''EXAMPLE_VAR\s*='''

findings.fix_hint:
Add a `.gitleaks.toml` (or `.secrets.baseline` for detect-secrets) and
wire it into pre-commit or a GitHub Actions workflow. Example:
https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks-action.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: .gitleaks.toml, .gitleaks.yaml, .gitleaks.yml, gitleaks.toml, .trufflehog.yaml, .trufflehog.yml, .secrets.baseline, .pre-commit-config.yaml, .github/workflows/gitleaks.yml, .github/workflows/secret-scan.yml

checks.explanation:
Tracked files containing AWS access keys, GitHub tokens, Slack
tokens, Google API keys, Stripe live keys, or PEM private keys
are almost always a credential leak. Even rotating after the fact
doesn't help — the leaked secret is in git history forever. This
rule catches the high-confidence patterns; for full coverage,
layer a tool like `gitleaks` (see safety.gitleaks_config) on top.

A SECURITY.md (or .github/SECURITY.md / docs/SECURITY.md) tells
agents and reporters where to send vulnerability disclosures and what
the project's response window is. Without it, a coding agent that
finds a security issue has no documented escalation path and may
default to opening a public issue.

action.template:
# Security policy

## Reporting a vulnerability

Email security@example.com or open a GitHub Security Advisory.
We respond within 48 hours and aim to ship a fix within 14 days.

findings.fix_hint:
Add a SECURITY.md at the repo root with at least: how to report a
vulnerability (private channel), supported versions, and expected
response time. GitHub's "Add a security policy" template is fine.
findings.message: None of these expected paths exist: SECURITY.md, .github/SECURITY.md, docs/SECURITY.md

verify.description: SECURITY.md must exist at the repo root.
